I agree we could try and simplify the repetitive calls to `int()` and `round()`. Unfortunately it is not that straightforward.

(1) For a start, it should be `best[1:5]`, however `best` is a [structured array](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/user/basics.rec.html) so it cannot be sliced like that.

I tried `best[['yc', 'xc', 'a', 'b']]` as proposed in the documentation, but I still get and "invalid index" error.

(2) Then comes the difference between [`round`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#round) and [`np.round`](http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.around.html):

```python
round(0.5) = 1.0
np.round(0.5) = 0.0
```

Personally, I would favor the Python `round` more than the Numpy one. If you look at the two images in my first post, using `np.round` produces to the old output (`int()`) while `round` corresponds to the proposed modification.

As a trade-off between condensed code and respecting all the constraints, I would suggest:

```python
yc, xc, a, b = map( lambda x: int(round(x)), best.tolist()[1:5] )
```

Let me know if you manage to handle the structured arrays in a better way, or if you prefer `np.round` other the default `round`.

I would naively interpret "longer than" to mean "has more observations than", but it looks to me like the actual behavior here is that we remove any values from `rets` that are older than the oldest observation in `benchmark_rets`. I also wouldn't know what "limit" means without more context.

I would describe the currently-implemented behavior here as something like:

```python
def clip_returns_to_benchmark(rets, benchmark_rets):
    """Drop entries from rets that precede the first entry in benchmark_rets.
    """
```

That said, is there a reason we're only clipping the left side here? Naively, I'd expect to have similar problems if rets/benchmark_rets don't align in either direction.

The thing is that currently `data` can only be displayed on the reports if it is one of the following (quoted from `annotate` docstring in `utils.py`):

 - 'number' : 'data' is a single number which can be an integer or
          float and should be represented as such.
 - 'count' : 'data' is a list, set or tuple. Choosing 'count' will
          display the length of that list e.g. number of metabolites without formula.
 - 'percent' : Instead of 'data' the content of 'metric' ought to be
          displayed e.g. percentage of metabolites without charge.
          'metric' is expected to be a floating point number.
- 'raw' : 'data' is ought to be displayed "as is" without formatting.
          This option is appropriate for single strings or a boolean output.

I actually don't think this belongs here (nor the start_logging call) -- it is already done in the ``run`` method and calling ``start`` on a component is (at least intended as) the lower-level API. That is, if you're already controlling your own event-loop. In this case, I would presume most users would also be controlling the log-starting themselves, too.

The idea is that "most" users should just call ``run([component0, component1])`` but if you wanted more control you'd call start yourself and deal with the deferreds/futures -- adding one more line in this case for ``start_logging`` yourself seems reasonable (since you'd also have to arrange for starting the event-loop anyway).

I interpret the `cc @nikhaas` from Max as a hinto to give my 2cents.

First of all, I see that you are only interested in "well behaved" frequencies, because you discard the last entry if `fft_abs` has an odd number of items. From the docs of [`np.fft.rfft`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.fft.rfft.html), we see that this last frequency is complex in general.

Your [reference from wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectral_centroid) seems only meaningful in the time domain. But as always with DFT, we carry no information about the time domain. I've also seen a [slightly different implementation](https://gist.github.com/endolith/359724/aa7fcc043776f16f126a0ccd12b599499509c3cc), where the author uses `np.arange(0,1,len(x)//2)` instead of `np.arange(0, len(x)//2, 1)`. But I prefer your implementation because of the association to the bin. Transforming back in the time domain is done by `val * seconds * sampling_freq / bin_no`.

However, I agree with Max: We could normalize all DFT values which yields a discrete probability distribution of the bins and then calculate some moments (if this is useful at all).

> I am not sure I get what you mean. 
Sorry, I'll try to explain my point more clearly this time :)

> The problem is that there is only one generic fit method in which EM steps are conducted and that's why I call _is_identifiable in fit method.

I see two ways to overcome this problem:

1. Add `_is_identifiable` property to `_BaseHMM`. The property should be `True` by default. The subclasses are free to override it and check the parameters for identifiability. This in fact is a slight modification of what you're proposing in the PR. The only difference is that `_is_identifiable` is a property, not a method. 
2. Do not change `_BaseHMM` and add diagnostic step to `_m_step` of the relevant models. The method is expected to issue a warning if parameters aren't identifiable.

The former way implies that EM iterates until it finds identifiable parameters. While in theory this might help with pathological cases, I'm not sure it is very useful in practice. Can you give an example of the dataset where EM first converges to equal means and then after a number of iterations uncovers the true means?

P. S. There's also a problem of defining `equals` for real-valued parameters. For some applications `0.0001` is equal to `0.00012` for other not. And since we are operating in floating point we **have** to pick the threshold somehow.

For Python 2 you want `if isinstance(reports, unicode)` (not `(str, unicode)`), 
for python 3 you want `if isinstance(reports, str)`.

So the correct fix would be:

```python
try:
    unicode
except NameError:
    # Must be Python 3, make an alias for str.
    unicode = str
if isinstance(reports, unicode):
    reports = reports.encode('utf-8')
```

However, Python 2 has `bytes` being an alias for `str`. So you can do:

```python
if not isinstance(reports, bytes):
    reports = reports.encode('utf-8')
```

I think we may want to address it now. On Debian based systems, we're incorrectly marking vhosts as enabled which can cause problems later in execution.

For example, while it's not trivial to repro, starting with the default Apache config on Ubuntu 16.04, do the following:
```
sudo a2dissite 000-default.conf
sudo mkdir /etc/apache2/sites-available2
sudo cp /etc/apache2/sites-available/000-default.conf /etc/apache2/sites-available2/
# Add a ServerName directive to /etc/apache2/sites-available2/000-default.conf that you can obtain a cert for
sudo certbot -d foo.science --apache --no-redirect --apache-vhost-root /etc/apache2/sites-available2/
# Add a ServerAlias directive to /etc/apache2/sites-available2/000-default.conf
sudo certbot -d foo.science --apache --redirect --apache-vhost-root /etc/apache2/sites-available2/
```
On this branch, this last command fails because it things the disabled vhost in `/etc/apache2/sites-available2` would conflict with a redirect vhost. If you checkout `master`, this problem goes away.

No, we don't and let the license unknown.
We don't chose a license for the producer because if we chose something wrong (ie. a license that is more permissive that the original one), we are legaly responsible.
Example: there is some bug differences between cc-by-sa 2.0, 3.0 et 4.0 but if someone type cc-by-sa 5.0 (which doesn't exists), I don't want to choose arbitrary between the different versions.

This is a first implementation but we already discussed some possible improvements for some other existing case:
- store a list of known synonyms for each license (ie. License Etalab for License ouverte)
- have some rule for license version handling (ie. CC-BY-SA without version is latest 4.0 version)
